title: Ease Jaw Pain with 10 of the Best Herbal Teas
intro_desc: Herbal teas for jaw pain are a natural remedy that helps alleviate discomfort and inflammation associated with temporomandibular joint (TMJ) disorders. These teas work by reducing stress, calming muscles, and promoting relaxation. The best herbal teas for jaw pain include Zingiber officinale, Echinacea purpurea, Lavandula angustifolia, Camellia sinensis, Achillea millefolium, Glycyrrhiza glabra, Valeriana officinalis, Salvia officinalis, Melissa officinalis, and Salvia miltiorrhiza. If left untreated, jaw pain can significantly impact daily life, causing difficulty eating, sleeping, and concentrating, leading to isolation, decreased productivity, and a reduced quality of life.

remedies.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale tea, also known as ginger tea, is often used to help with jaw pain. This is because ginger has natural anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce swelling and pain in the jaw. When you have a headache or jaw pain, the blood vessels in your face can get bigger, which can lead to pain and discomfort. Ginger tea can help reduce this swelling by releasing chemicals that relax blood vessels and reduce pain. It can also help with digestion and nausea, which can sometimes be related to jaw pain. Drinking ginger tea may also help to reduce muscle tension in the jaw, which can lead to pain and stiffness. Additionally, ginger tea contains compounds that have antioxidant properties, which can help protect your body from damage caused by free radicals, and may also help to reduce pain and inflammation.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Gingerols are not actually found in this plant; however, the plant does contain Quinine, which is commonly used to treat malaria and other conditions, and has been known to relieve toothache and jaw pain by numbing the affected area.

remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Cinchona officinalis herbal tea for jaw pain relief, start by gathering 1-2 teaspoons of dried Cinchona bark, which is available at most health food stores or online. Next, boil a cup of water in a teapot or kettle, and then reduce the heat to a simmer. Add the dried Cinchona bark to the water and let it steep for 5-7 minutes, allowing the herbs to infuse into the liquid. Strain the tea into a cup using a tea strainer or a piece of cheesecloth to catch the bark particles. You can add honey or lemon to taste, but be sure to drink the tea while it's still warm, as the heat from the water helps to release the active compounds from the Cinchona bark. Once you've made the tea, drink it slowly to allow the compounds to take effect, and you may start to feel relief from your jaw pain within 15-30 minutes. It's essential to note that Cinchona officinalis tea may interact with certain medications, so consult with a healthcare professional before using it to treat jaw pain.

remedies.remedy_desc: Echinacea purpurea tea is often used to help with jaw pain because it has anti-inflammatory properties. This means it can reduce swelling and ease pain in the jaw. The tea contains compounds called alkylamides and glycosides, which are thought to help reduce inflammation. Inflammation is often the cause of pain and stiffness in the jaw, especially when we have conditions like temporomandibular joint (TMJ) disorder. When we drink Echinacea purpurea tea, these compounds can help calm down the inflammation in the jaw, making it feel better. The tea also has antispasmodic properties, which means it can help relax the muscles in the jaw, reducing pain and stiffness. Many people find that drinking Echinacea purpurea tea helps to reduce their jaw pain and improve their overall comfort.

Quercetin is good for jaw pain because it has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce swelling and ease pain in the jaw joint and surrounding areas.

remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Cinchona officinalis herbal tea that helps with jaw pain, start by gathering the necessary ingredients: dried Cinchona officinalis bark, a tea infuser or strainer, a cup, and boiling water. Begin by measuring one teaspoon of dried Cinchona officinalis bark for every cup of water you are using. Place the measured bark in the tea infuser or strainer and set it aside. Next, boil fresh water in a kettle or on the stovetop. Once the water has reached a rolling boil, carefully pour it over the Cinchona officinalis bark in the infuser or strainer. Allow the mixture to steep for 5-7 minutes, depending on your desired tea strength. After the steeping time has passed, carefully lift the infuser or strainer out of the cup and discard the bark. Your Cinchona officinalis tea is now ready to drink. To help with jaw pain, drink the tea 2-3 times a day, either on its own or with a little honey to sweeten it. Be sure to check with a healthcare professional before using any new herbal remedy, especially if you have any underlying medical conditions or are taking medications.

remedies.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra tea, also known as licorice root tea, is often used to help with jaw pain. The active compound in licorice root, called glycyrrhizin, has anti-inflammatory properties. This means it can reduce swelling and ease pain in the jaw. Jaw pain, also known as temporomandibular joint (TMJ) disorder, is often caused by inflammation or irritation in the jaw joint. Drinking licorice root tea may help to calm down this inflammation and provide relief. Additionally, glycyrrhizin may also help to relax the muscles in the jaw, which can contribute to pain. Some people also find that licorice root tea helps to soothe digestive issues, which can be related to jaw pain. Drinking a cup of licorice root tea may be a natural way to try and alleviate jaw pain.

You can purchase this at a local health food store or online. Next, boil 1 cup of water and let it cool for a minute to prevent the bark from becoming bitter. Then, add the Cinchona bark to the hot water and let it steep for 5-7 minutes. Strain the liquid using a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a cup, and discard the Cinchona bark. You can add honey or lemon to taste, but be sure to use a small amount as Cinchona bark has a strong flavor. Drink the tea 2-3 times a day to help alleviate jaw pain caused by inflammation or TMJ disorders. It's essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using Cinchona bark, especially if you're taking any medications or have underlying health conditions.

remedies.remedy_desc: Ginkgo biloba tea is a popular remedy for jaw pain. This is because the leaves of the Ginkgo biloba tree contain special compounds that help to relax and reduce inflammation in the body. When we have jaw pain, it's often due to tension and swelling in the muscles of the jaw. Ginkgo biloba tea works by reducing this tension and inflammation, which helps to ease the pain. The tea also contains antioxidants that help to protect the body from damage caused by free radicals, which can contribute to pain and inflammation. By drinking Ginkgo biloba tea regularly, people may find that their jaw pain becomes less frequent and less severe. The tea is also easy to make and is often combined with other herbs to enhance its benefits. Some people find it helpful to drink Ginkgo biloba tea before going to bed, as it can help to relax the jaw muscles and promote a good night's sleep.

Rinse the bark under cold running water to remove any dirt, then chop it into small pieces. In a large pot, bring 2 cups of water to a boil. Add 1 tablespoon of chopped Cinchona bark and let it simmer for 5-7 minutes, allowing the active compounds to release into the water. Remove the pot from the heat and let it steep for another 10-15 minutes. Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup, discarding the solids. Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired. Let the tea cool down before drinking it. It's recommended to drink 1-2 cups of Cinchona tea per day for relief from jaw pain.

remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Cinchona officinalis herbal tea that helps with jaw pain, start by gathering high-quality Cinchona bark, which is the part used for medicinal purposes. Rinse the bark under cold running water to remove any dirt or debris, then pat it dry with a clean towel. Next, place one teaspoon of the dried bark into a tea infuser or a small piece of cheesecloth, depending on your preference. Bring a cup of water to a boil, then reduce the heat to a simmer and place the tea infuser or cheesecloth into the water. Allow the Cinchona bark to steep for 5-7 minutes, releasing its active compounds into the water. Strain the tea into a cup and discard the bark. You can add honey or lemon to taste, but be sure to use a minimal amount of honey as Cinchona bark is quite bitter. Once the tea has cooled slightly, use it as a rinse for your jaw or drink it as needed to help alleviate pain and inflammation.

supplementary_best: Combining Zingiber officinale (ginger), Glycyrrhiza glabra (licorice root), and Salvia officinalis (sage) may help remedy jaw pain. Ginger's anti-inflammatory properties can reduce swelling and ease pain. Licorice root's anti-inflammatory and pain-relieving properties can further help alleviate jaw discomfort. Sage contains compounds that may relax facial muscles and improve blood circulation, which can reduce tension and promote healing in the jaw area. This combination of herbs may provide a comprehensive approach to managing jaw pain by addressing inflammation, pain, and muscle relaxation.
supplementary_avoid: If you have jaw pain, you may want to avoid herbal teas that can irritate your teeth and gums or make jaw problems worse. One of these teas is Peppermint tea, made from 'Mentha piperita'. The menthol in Peppermint can numb the jaw and face, which might make jaw pain feel worse.  Another tea to be cautious with is Sage tea, made from 'Sage' or 'Salvia officinalis'. While Sage is known for its soothing properties, it can also cause jaw pain in some people by relaxing the muscles that hold the jaw in place.  Eucalyptus tea, made from 'Eucalyptus globulus', also needs to be used with care. The menthol in Eucalyptus, similar to Peppermint, can numb the jaw and face, making jaw pain feel worse.  Lastly, 'Lavandula angustifolia' or Lavender tea should be consumed with moderation as some people experience an allergic reaction, which could lead to an increase in jaw pain.
supplementary_other_remedies: Cold compresses can help with jaw pain by reducing inflammation and numbing the area. Applying a cold compress to the affected jaw area can help slow down the flow of blood, which in turn reduces the swelling and ease the pain. This is because cold temperatures cause the blood vessels to constrict, or get smaller, which reduces the amount of blood flowing to the area. The cold sensation also numbs the nerves, making it harder for pain signals to be sent to the brain.  Warm compresses can also be helpful, especially if the pain is due to a muscle spasm or a sinus infection. Heat helps relax the muscles and increase blood flow, which can help ease the pain and reduce tension. This is because heat causes the blood vessels to dilate, or get bigger, which increases the amount of blood flowing to the area. The warmth can also help loosen up any mucus in the sinuses, making it easier to breathe and reducing the pressure on the jaw.  Epsom salt baths can also be beneficial for jaw pain, especially if the pain is due to stress or tension. Epsom salt contains magnesium, which is a natural muscle relaxant that can help ease muscle cramps and spasms. When added to warm water, the magnesium from the Epsom salt is absorbed through the skin, helping to relax the muscles and reduce pain.  Olive oil massage can also be helpful for jaw pain, especially if the pain is due to a tension headache or a temporomandibular joint (TMJ) disorder. Massaging the jaw area with olive oil can help relax the muscles and increase blood flow, which can help ease the pain and reduce tension. Olive oil also contains anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce swelling and inflammation in the affected area.
supplementary_related_ailments: People with jaw pain often experience other ailments due to the close connection between the jaw, face, and head muscles. One common issue is migraines and headaches, which can be relieved with feverfew, a herb that blocks pain-causing chemicals. Ginger is another helpful herb for jaw pain and headaches, as it reduces inflammation and relaxes tense muscles.  Tension and stress can also lead to jaw pain, as well as anxiety and depression. Passionflower is a calming herb that can help alleviate these issues by promoting relaxation and reducing anxiety.  Bad posture, often a result of chronic jaw pain, can lead to back and neck pain. Epsom salt baths with arnica can help relax these muscles and reduce pain.  Additionally, jaw pain can be linked to digestive issues, such as irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), due to the close connection between the jaw and digestive system. Peppermint oil can help ease digestive issues by reducing inflammation and relaxing stomach muscles.  Stress and tension can also cause ear problems, such as tinnitus, due to the close connection between the jaw and ear muscles. Ginkgo biloba can help reduce tinnitus by improving blood flow to the ears.
